roots.title: Introduction to Visualization Theory and Homework 3 Details
roots.summary: The segment covers visualization theory, emphasizing visual system sensitivity and adaptation of visualizations. Homework 3 is announced, supported by a $50,000 investment, and will be available for 10 to 12 days. Students are advised to manage DataHub resource limitations and avoid multiple tabs to prevent kernel restarts. The importance of visualization in modeling and stakeholder communication is highlighted, with upcoming topics including visualization principles and data transformation techniques. The process for building and evaluating linear regression models is outlined, covering question formulation, data acquisition, exploratory data analysis (EDA), and reporting. Distribution plotting techniques, particularly histograms and the seaborn library, are reviewed, focusing on how distributions summarize data trends. The relationship between Gross National Income (GNI) per capita and adult literacy rates is explored, noting that higher GNI correlates with improved literacy. Generalizability in data modeling is discussed, indicating that vertical data distribution shows minimal impact of GNI on illiteracy rates, necessitating data transformation for linear regression. Recommended linearization techniques include logarithmic and polynomial transformations.

children.title: Introduction to Visualization Theory and Homework 3 Details
children.summary: The segment introduces visualization theory, focusing on the evaluation of visual system sensitivity and adaptation of visualizations. Homework 3 is announced, supported by a $50,000 departmental investment, and will be available for 10 to 12 days. Students are advised to be patient with DataHub cells that may run slowly due to limited resources and to avoid opening the assignment in multiple tabs to prevent kernel restarts, especially with NLP-related cells. The importance of visualization and data transformation in modeling and stakeholder communication is emphasized, with upcoming topics including visualization principles, transformation techniques, probabilities, data distribution, and sampling frames. The process of building and evaluating linear regression models is outlined, detailing steps such as question formulation, data acquisition, prediction, exploratory data analysis (EDA), and the importance of reporting decisions and solutions. Additionally, distribution plotting techniques are reviewed, focusing on histograms and the seaborn library, explaining how distributions summarize data trends and the analysis of frequency within bins, along with options for density plots and kernel density estimates (KDE).

children.title: Introduction to Visualization Theory and Homework 3 Details
children.summary: The lecture introduces visualization theory, emphasizing the evaluation of the visual system's sensitivity and the adaptation of visualizations. An important announcement regarding Homework 3 highlights a $50,000 investment by the department, with the assignment available for 10 to 12 days. Students are reminded to be patient with certain cells in DataHub that may take a long time to run due to limited computing resources. They should avoid opening the assignment in multiple tabs to prevent issues and unnecessary kernel restarts, particularly with cells related to the Natural Language Processing (NLP) model, which may also take longer to execute.

children.title: Introduction to Visualization Theory and Homework 3 Details
children.summary: The lecture introduces visualization theory, focusing on evaluating the sensitivity of the visual system and adapting visualizations accordingly. An important announcement regarding Homework 3 highlights its significance and the $50,000 investment made by the department for this assignment, which will be available for approximately 10 to 12 days. Additionally, students are reminded to be patient with certain cells in DataHub that may take a long time to run due to finite computing resources, and to avoid opening the assignment in multiple tabs to prevent issues.

children.title: Homework 03 Logistics and NLP Model Considerations
children.summary: Students will work with different language models to understand sentiment analysis and embeddings. Due to limited computing resources on DataHub, certain cells using the NLP model may take a long time to run. Students are advised to be patient, avoid restarting the kernel unnecessarily, and not to open the assignment in multiple tabs or windows to prevent crashes.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Some language models in order to get the sentiment of different different sentences and different articles. So it's going to be really fun. We wanted to give you this experience of working with different language models to make sense of how they work and how they are actually doing the embeddings or evaluations based on the small tweaks and changes that you're making in the sentences. So because this homework is very expensive, especially because at the time that you're looting the model and you're doing the inference on the model, there are some considerations that you have to have so that we can keep data hub active because last semester, the data hub basically crashed the data hub for almost a day, a pencil deploying that we could bump up the resources and then restart the homework. So that doesn't happen. One, all of you to think about all of the precautions that we have here. Video: 1: v

children.title: Important Note on Homework 03 Logistics
children.summary: Students are advised to be patient while running cells in the homework assignment, especially those involving Natural Language Processing (NLP) models, as they may take a long time to execute. It is crucial not to restart the kernel or rerun cells unnecessarily. Additionally, students should avoid opening the assignment in multiple tabs or windows to prevent crashing and to stay within the budget of computing resources allocated for 1200 students.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Know that each one of the cells that you're running, they might take a long time for those cells to run. Don't rerun stuff. Just wait patiently until you get the output of each one of the cells. So don't rerun stuff. Also, the budget that we have is going to be depending on each number of windows, perhaps, that are open on your laptop, right? So if you want to work on the homework, only open it in one tap. don't open it in multiple taps, because the number of taps that you're creating, it's like as if we have more students working on that homework and each one of those taps will be paying. And then it's not that the pay is that important, it's also important that our budget is only for 1200 students. So if all of you opens up a second tab, it's as if that we have 2400 students and we don't have budget for it, right? Budget in terms of resource allocation and the date and then we might crash later how they get. Video: 1: v

children.title: Important Notes on Homework 03 Logistics
children.summary: Students are advised to be patient with Homework 03, as certain cells that import or use the Natural Language Processing model may take a long time to run due to limited computing resources on DataHub. It is recommended to avoid restarting the kernel or rerunning cells unnecessarily, and to refrain from opening the assignment in multiple tabs or windows to prevent notebook crashes and resource allocation issues.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So don't reopen, wait for each one of the cells to run as usually it will take a long time. And then also a lot of time, we also wanna make sure that you don't restart the kernel and run everything over and over again. Only restart kernel, only if you need it because every time that you run everything, end to end, it will cost us. It will allocate resources to you and then it will cost us. Question in the box. This is just, I mean, generally the amount of resources that you're going to allocate, that you're going to basically use is depending on the number of tabs that you have open. But because all of the other homeworks are cheaper in a sense and they don't need resources that much, so it's not going to be that much of a concern for us. It's just going to be a couple of bucks more per student. But this one is going to be very expensive if you all start doing that. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

children.title: Important Note on Homework 03 Resources
children.summary: Homework 03 involves a language model hosted locally, which requires careful resource management due to its high computational demands. It is noted as the most expensive homework in the course. Students are advised to be patient with certain cells that may take a long time to run and to avoid restarting the kernel or rerunning cells unnecessarily. Opening the assignment in multiple tabs or windows is discouraged as it may lead to notebook crashes and affect DataHub performance.

children.title: Overview of Visualization, Data Transformation, and Linear Regression Models
children.summary: The lecture covers the significance of visualization and data transformation in building relationships between variables, emphasizing their importance for modeling and stakeholder communication. Upcoming topics include visualization principles, transformation techniques, probabilities, data distribution, and sampling frames, with a focus on modeling in later weeks. Additionally, it discusses the process of building and evaluating linear regression models, detailing steps in data analysis such as question formulation, data acquisition, prediction, exploratory data analysis (EDA), and the necessity of reporting decisions and solutions.

children.title: Overview of Visualization and Data Transformation
children.summary: The lecture focuses on building relationships between variables through visualization and understanding necessary transformations for data preparation. Emphasis is placed on the importance of data transformation throughout the semester, as it is crucial for modeling and effective communication with stakeholders. The upcoming topics include visualization principles, transformation techniques, probabilities, data distribution, and sampling frames, with a deeper dive into modeling in subsequent weeks.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So with that added away, we are going into visualization too. So this is the second lecture in this sequence. What I'm planning to do is to go a little bit slower, slower today compared to what we've seen in the previous lectures, kind of and kind of pick a look at how we can build relationships or visualize relationships between variables. And then how can we make sense of different types of transformations that we need to have in order to have a very solid data set and solid visualization moving forward in the modeling and also sharing that broadly in your reports or sharing it with the stakeholders. At the end, we're going to comment, talk a little bit about visualization theory, how you can use different information channels on the plot more effectively. And then we'll talk about that at the end. So again, we're going to talk about some kind of visualization principle, then we're going to talk about transformation. And transformation is really important. This is the topic that you will keep seeing throughout the semester, because we'll give you different data sets. You need to make your data sets ready for the modeling piece. Every time you need to think about whether you need to transform your data or not. So make sure that you are solid on understanding the principles behind transformation and why we do transformation before we jump into the modeling phase. Kind of looking ahead in the next week we're going to come back and talk a little bit about Probabilities and understanding the distribution understanding the sampling frame in your data set and then a week after I'm going to come back and start the modeling lectures. So we're going to De kind of Go deeper into understanding that okay, not that your data is ready. Video: 1: v

Kind of looking ahead in the next week we're going to come back and talk a little bit about Probabilities and understanding the distribution understanding the sampling frame in your data set and then a week after I'm going to come back and start the modeling lectures. So we're going to De kind of Go deeper into understanding that okay, not that your data is ready.

children.title: Overview of Distribution Plotting Techniques and Visualization
children.summary: The segment reviews distribution plotting techniques, focusing on histograms and the seaborn library's plot function. It explains distributions as summaries of data trends, detailing the transformation of quantitative data into bins and analyzing frequency within those bins. Y-axis options for histograms, including density plots representing probability distributions, are discussed. Kernel density estimates (KDE) are briefly mentioned, describing the application of kernels to data points. The 'displot' function is introduced as a tool for visualizing distributions, showcasing gross national income (GNI) per capita through histogram, KDE, and empirical cumulative distribution function (ECDF) plots. The empirical CDF illustrates the probability of data points being below a specific value. Additionally, scatter plots are emphasized for modeling relationships between variables, revealing trends crucial for understanding before applying linear regression.

children.title: Overview of Distribution Plotting Techniques
children.summary: The segment reviews previous content on plotting distributions, specifically focusing on histograms and the seaborn library's plot function. It explains that a distribution summarizes the trend in data by showing unique values and their frequencies. The discussion includes transforming quantitative data into categories (bins) and analyzing the frequency of occurrences within those bins. Additionally, it covers y-axis options for histograms, including density plots, which represent probability distributions where the area under the curve equals 100% or 1. The segment also briefly mentions kernel density estimates (KDE), describing how kernels are applied to data points to model the distribution.

So, talking about our agenda, the first thing is kind of reminding you of what we have seen in a previous lecture. Basically, in a previous lecture, we were just trying to plot distribution. We had seen that we can plot histograms, there were different ways that we could plot histograms. But the last thing that I left you with was that this plot function in seaborn that's kind of a wrapper on top of any kind of visualization that you want to have, on plotting distributions. I remember that the distribution was a way for us to summarize the entire trend in the data. We would basically say that how many unique values we have and how many of each one of those unique values we have, that's a distribution. When we're looking at quantitative variable, we're basically looking at the scale and we're trying to bend our data points into different categories, kind of transforming your quantitative data to a semi-qualitative data and the categories be all of those bins. I'm going to be looking at the frequency of the occurrences of values in each one of those bins, essentially. So this is an example of us. Flooding the histogram and we do that by calling this blood and specifying that the kind is his. And on the y-axis we talked about we can plot different things on the y-axis. We can plot density. If you plot density that means that the area under the curve or the area, the summation of the area for all of those bars should add up to be equal to 100% or be equal to 1 so that it generally shows a probability distribution. We could also plot a KDE plot. KDE is then for a kernel-lensity estimate. This is what we saw in the previous lecture. Basically, on each one of the data points, we would put the kernel. We call that the kernel could be any probability function that models one specific data point. We put the kernel on all of the data points. We scale them down. We sum them up so that it gives us this kernel-lensity estimate curve. But basically what it is is that it's an estimation of the trend that you see in the histogram. children.title: Using displot for Distribution Visualization
children.summary: The segment discusses the use of the 'displot' function in data visualization, which serves as a wrapper for 'histplot', 'kdeplot', and 'ecdfplot' to effectively plot distributions. It specifically illustrates how to visualize the distribution of gross national income (GNI) per capita using different types of plots: histogram, kernel density estimate (KDE), and empirical cumulative distribution function (ECDF). Examples are provided for each plot type, showcasing the distribution and cumulative distribution of GNI.

children.title: Empirical CDF and Scatter Plots in Data Analysis
children.summary: The segment discusses the empirical cumulative distribution function (ECDF), which illustrates the probability that a data point is below or equal to a specific value, exemplified by 85% of data points being below 40,000 GNI. It transitions to modeling relationships between multiple variables, emphasizing scatter plots as a tool to visualize associations between two variables (x1 and x2). The importance of scatter plots lies in their ability to reveal trends, whether linear, non-linear, or semi-linear, which is crucial for understanding relationships before applying linear regression for prediction.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: started okay we can also go and plot the ECDF. chart, which is empirical cumulative distribution function, which shows that at every given point, for example, here, what's the ratio or what's the What's the probability that any data point is below or equal to that specific point, which in this case is 85% or something of our data points are below, for example, 40,000 GNI. So that's kind of how we try to plot distributions. But moving away from distributions, what if we want to model the relationship between multiple different variables as opposed to is just looking at one variable. So what we can do is something that you had seen before in data 8. In data 8, you had seen a scatter plots. And scatter plots were kind of showing that what's the association between two different variables. So for example, here I call this variable x1, this variable, or this dimension x2. And for every given data point, it's the relationship between x1 and x2 that you're plotting as a single point on the plot. Usually with a scatter plot, we could basically plot the entire data set or the association between X1 and X2 variables, and it will show us the trend. So in this case, for example, you're seeing that I have a simple linear kind of trend in the data. I can also observe non-linear trend. I can also see kind of a semi-linear trend, but it's kind of spreading as X grows or kind of V shape relationship between two variables. Why is it important? to visualize two variables with respect to each other. It's mostly important because when we go into linear regression and we have a target variable that we want to predict. And we want to predict that using some input information or some input feature. We might want to visualize it in order to see if they actually for fact have linear relationship with each other or not. So that's part of the reason that we might want to use kind of visualization of the relationship between two different variables in a sense. So how do we plot the scatter plots? You've seen map plot lab, you've seen seaborne. Effectively, the structure of how you can call each one of the plotting functions in map plot lab and seaborne is very similar. So remember that what we had was with the map plot lab, we could just call the plotting function and we would pass the sequences that need to be plotted. So for example, in this case, I have, I can pass a sequence, which means it can be an array, it can be a series, it can be a list object of x values and y values that need will be plotted. children.title: Scatter Plots: Definition and Usage
children.summary: Scatter plots are utilized to illustrate the relationship between two quantitative variables. One variable is plotted on the x-axis and the other on the y-axis, with each point on the scatter plot representing an individual data point from the dataset. The segment includes examples of how to create scatter plots using Python, specifically with the commands plt.scatter for basic plotting and sns.scatterplot for enhanced visualization with additional parameters like hue.

children.title: Creating and Understanding Scatter Plots
children.summary: Scatter plots are utilized to illustrate relationships between two quantitative variables by plotting one variable on the x-axis and the other on the y-axis. Each point on the scatter plot represents a single data point from the dataset. The syntax for creating scatter plots includes 'plt.scatter(x_values, y_values)' for basic plotting and 'sns.scatterplot(data=df, x="x_column", y="y_column", hue="hue_column")' for enhanced visualization that incorporates a third dimension through color coding based on categorical values.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: You just need to remember about plotting function. What's the name of that plotting function that will do the thing that you want? So here in this case is the scatter plot. column name, so that's the string value. The column name that goes on the x-axis, the column name that goes on the y-axis. And then if you're feeling fancy, we could also say that there is a third dimension that I want you to plot and that's the color. So, hue references the color, which basically means that, okay, depending on different categories of values that you have in the column that's called hue column, go ahead and color different stuff. children.title: Scatter Plots: Relationships Between Variables
children.summary: Scatter plots are utilized to illustrate relationships between two quantitative variables by plotting one on the x-axis and the other on the y-axis. Each point on the scatter plot represents a single data point from the dataset. The segment includes examples of how to create scatter plots using Python libraries, specifically 'plt.scatter' for basic plotting and 'sns.scatterplot' for enhanced visualizations, including the use of a hue parameter to differentiate data points.

children.title: Creating Scatter Plots with Seaborn
children.summary: Scatter plots are utilized to illustrate relationships between two quantitative variables by plotting one variable on the x-axis and the other on the y-axis. Each point on the scatter plot represents an individual data point from the dataset. The syntax for creating a scatter plot using Seaborn includes the function sns.scatterplot, where 'data' specifies the dataset, 'x' and 'y' denote the respective columns for the x and y axes, and 'hue' can be used to differentiate data points by category, such as continent.

children.title: Introduction to Scatter Plots and Overplotting Issues
children.summary: Scatter plots are introduced as a method to reveal relationships between two quantitative variables, with one variable plotted on the x-axis and the other on the y-axis. Each point on the scatter plot represents a data point from the dataset. The discussion highlights the issue of overplotting, where data points cluster together, making it difficult to identify trends, particularly in high-density areas of the plot. Examples of plotting techniques, including the use of libraries like matplotlib and seaborn, are mentioned to illustrate how to create scatter plots effectively.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: This is kind of the plot that I'll get. And while keep working on this plot, we'll see different issues with this plot. We'll try to solve it, and then we'll try to also summarize all of these trends that you see using other types of plots. And then we'll see what kind of events, kind of similar plots that we can use in order to show the relationship between two different variables. How are we doing so far? We reviewed a... the how we can plot distribution or the trend in one column and now we're going into reviewing what how we can plot two variables with respect to each other and we're starting with a scatter plot. So what we had in the previous plot was that there was one major issue and you'll keep hearing from me that I'll say that okay this plot has a lot of overplotting I can see this is not a good plot. What overplotting essentially means is that if I go back to the previous slide you will see that there is a lot of these different points that are basically clumped together in that top right corner and I don't like that because I can't tell. tell apart what's happening in that area. It can figure out is there a specific trend for a specific continent or not? Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

children.title: Remedies for Overplotting in Scatter Plots
children.summary: Overplotting occurs when points in a scatter plot are plotted on top of each other, making it difficult to discern individual data points. One remedy for overplotting is to add random noise to the data points. This involves generating random values for both the x and y dimensions of the data, which helps to spread the points apart and reduce clutter in the visualization. The process includes creating a noise set for both dimensions and applying this noise when plotting the scatter plot, allowing for clearer representation of relationships between the two quantitative variables.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So it's just that I don't want to see points plotted on top of each other. So that's called overplotting. There are different remedies that we have for overplotting. There is one simple way is that we would just add random noise. If we add random noise, for example, in this case, I would say that I'm adding random noise to the X dimension of all of the data points. So you can see that for all of the data points, I'm looking at the lengths of WB data frame, which is essentially the number of rows that I have, right? in the in the word bank data frame that we started using in the last lecture and I'm also creating a noise kind of less or no set of values for the wide dimension and what we'll do here is that we'll say that as you're creating this a scatter plot please go ahead and the noise That we created on the x-axis to the x-dimension and also please add the noise that we created for the y-axis to the y-axis dimension and then plot the values. Okay, so the idea is that because we're adding noise and because noise is randomly generated from a certain distribution. It will help us. of move points apart from each other so that they're not exactly flooded on top of each other. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza X

children.title: Random Uniform Generator and Jittering Techniques
children.summary: A random generator function creates values with a uniform distribution between -1 and 1. To address overplotting in scatter plots, jittering is introduced by adding small random noise to the x and y values, making individual data points easier to distinguish while maintaining overall trends. An example demonstrates using numpy to generate noise and adjusting marker size in Matplotlib to improve visualization.

children.title: Overplotting and Jittering in Scatter Plots
children.summary: Overplotting occurs when scatter points overlap, making it difficult to see individual data points. To mitigate this, jittering can be applied, which involves adding a small amount of random noise to the x and y values of each point. This maintains the main trends while making individual data points more distinguishable. An example is provided using NumPy to generate random noise and Matplotlib to create a scatter plot with jittered values for female adult literacy rates and percent growth per capita.

children.title: Adjusting Plot Parameters for Clarity
children.summary: To improve data visualization, the y-axis range can be adjusted to be between -5 and 5, allowing for better differentiation in plots. Increasing the size of plot markers to 15 can help reduce overlapping points. While adding noise to the data can create inaccuracies, it can also help illustrate trends, provided a disclaimer is included to inform viewers of the adjustments. Additionally, the LM plot function is introduced, which combines a scatter plot with a linear regression line, aiding in visualizing the relationship between the X and Y dimensions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's where the x-axis for the y-axis I'm saying that now your wrench can be wider it's going between negative negative 5 and 5 because the range of the y-axis is slightly different than the range of the x-axis. We need to jitter a little bit more so that we see the differences in the plot. So that's basically what's happening. The other way in order to make sure that the points are not on top of each other is to change the marking size. So here I'm saying that the size of each one of those dots that you're putting, let that be 15. And 15 is a smaller than the normal, valid, and it says it will also help to some extent. But these are kind of general ideas that we're using. So that the plot that you're getting at the end, you can interpret it. So the question is then going to be that if you're adding in noise, is that going to create inacurus in the plot? Or if you're trying to kind of show some trend to a mistake holder by adding in noise, you're basically kind of... uh false define the the underlying data and the answer is yes sort of but if you do that uh and and this and kind of put a disclaimer that the data is jitter so that you don't have overplodding that's fine and then people would know that okay the data is not exactly as is plotted but we can basically see the trend of very majority of the data is plotted versus not. So, in an attempt to also understand the relationships better, and in order to also mitigate a little bit of this overplotting that we have, we also have some other plotting functions. So, the first plotting function that is very interesting and you're going to see that in linear regression lectures is called LM plot. So, it's basically a plot that plots the linear regression, it plots the scatter plot, and then it will also put the linear regression line through the data. It will assume that you want to use the X dimension in order to predict the Y dimension. It will find the trend, the linear trend, and it will put that. You're not saying that exactly, but there is this. kind of confidence interval or this cone area which will show you that okay this is the trend and this is our 95% confidence interval for where the data points could be if you're modeling the trend using this linear regression so those are kind of the lines and the small confidence interval that you see on the plot as well. So the function exactly you call LM plot, you pass the data from, you pass the columns that you want to be plotted, the names, and it will plot that for you. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

children.title: Understanding Joint Plots and Variants
children.summary: Joint plots are effective for visualizing joint distributions using scatter plots and histograms on individual axes. The standard joint plot features a central scatter plot with histograms for the x and y distributions at the corners, which helps in assessing data point density despite potential overplotting. The syntax for creating a joint plot involves using the joint plot function with the data frame and specified column names. Variations include the hex plot, which aggregates data points into hexagons, coloring them based on point density to provide a clearer view of data distribution.

children.title: Understanding Joint Plots and Variants
children.summary: Joint plots are useful for visualizing joint distributions through scatter plots and histograms of individual axes. The basic joint plot displays the scatter plot in the center with histograms of the x and y distributions on the corners, aiding in understanding data point density despite potential overplotting. The syntax involves calling the joint plot function with the data frame and column names. Additionally, there are variations like the hex plot, which groups data points into hexagons, coloring them based on the density of points, allowing for a clearer representation of data distribution.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The other interesting plot that I like is also joint plot. So joint plot, there are different versions of joint plot, kind of similar to this plot that you can plot different kinds of this plot. Here you can also plot different kinds of joint plot, but this is the most basic one. What it does is it will create the joint distribution using the scatter plot, and it will also plot the histogram of the distribution of the individual x-axis and individual y-axis on the corners as well. So here, for example, you're seeing that it shows the histogram or the distribution of the y-axis here on the y-scale, and then this is the distribution of x-axis on the x-axis. So this will help you to some extent figure out how many data points you have here. Although you have overplotting, but this will help you kind of see that, okay, now this is how many data points I have in that corner, and this is also how many data points I have in this top part of the plot. So this kind of helps you understand the individual distributions as well as the joint distribution using this scatter plot. So the syntax is exactly So you call joint plot, past the data frame, past the column names, and you'll be on your way. There are other different fancy versions of Joint Plot. So this is a Joint Plot where I can say that the kind of the thing that I want you to plot in the middle is not a scatter plot, but give me the hex plot. So here I'm saying that the kind is equal to hex. What it is is essentially, we'll try to clump. different data points into hexagons and then it will color hexagons based on the number of data points that are present in each one of those locations. So if we take a look at this previous part, you're seeing that I have a high distribution somewhere around here. And then this is the highest distribution and then slightly lower distribution here and then the lowest distribution is going to be here and here. So we all start with the dark blue in the hexagons where we have the most distribution and then lighter blue lighter blue up until yellow up until white when there is no data point present in those areas, which is what you're seeing we did not have any data point here so we don't have. The hexagons are colored as white and then we have majority of the distribution here so that's why it's a dark blue hexagon in those areas. So it will basically help you to avoid overplotting but it will also help you to see the distribution because now that the data points are not on top of each other and I'm just counting them and I'm coloring a region based on that. That's all you need as a that's what you need as a viewer of this plot just know the distribution. Using the same strategy, we could go a little bit fancier and instead of just doing the counting or doing the coloring based on the counts, we could do it based on the KVE. So assume that you have all of those points or different indicators for each one of these points in the 2D surface. You put a KDE plot on top of it and you color it based on, for example, the height of those KDE kind of or that KDE function, which essentially is going to tell you that, okay, this is where I have the tallest KDE kind of point or the curve and then as I'm going away into different then the color is going to be lighter blue and then in these areas I have none data point. So that's basically the point that the KDE curve is going to all the way to zero. Right? So this is a 2D KDE version of what we did have before. The way that you can implement it as exactly as what how we would call the KDE plot. Here I would say that I have a KDE plot, but instead of only passing one data point or one column, I would say that I have a data frame and I'm passing you two different columns. So just go ahead and plot it or plot the KDE plot in two dimension using all of these contours that we're seeing. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza X

leaf_chunk.audio: The other interesting plot that I like is also joint plot. So joint plot, there are different versions of joint plot, kind of similar to this plot that you can plot different kinds of this plot. Here you can also plot different kinds of joint plot, but this is the most basic one. What it does is it will create the joint distribution using the scatter plot, and it will also plot the histogram of the distribution of the individual x-axis and individual y-axis on the corners as well. So here, for example, you're seeing that it shows the histogram or the distribution of the y-axis here on the y-scale, and then this is the distribution of x-axis on the x-axis. So this will help you to some extent figure out how many data points you have here. Although you have overplotting, but this will help you kind of see that, okay, now this is how many data points I have in that corner, and this is also how many data points I have in this top part of the plot. So this kind of helps you understand the individual distributions as well as the joint distribution using this scatter plot. So the syntax is exactly So you call joint plot, past the data frame, past the column names, and you'll be on your way. There are other different fancy versions of Joint Plot. So this is a Joint Plot where I can say that the kind of the thing that I want you to plot in the middle is not a scatter plot, but give me the hex plot. So here I'm saying that the kind is equal to hex. What it is is essentially, we'll try to clump. different data points into hexagons and then it will color hexagons based on the number of data points that are present in each one of those locations. So if we take a look at this previous part, you're seeing that I have a high distribution somewhere around here. And then this is the highest distribution and then slightly lower distribution here and then the lowest distribution is going to be here and here. So we all start with the dark blue in the hexagons where we have the most distribution and then lighter blue lighter blue up until yellow up until white when there is no data point present in those areas, which is what you're seeing we did not have any data point here so we don't have. The hexagons are colored as white and then we have majority of the distribution here so that's why it's a dark blue hexagon in those areas. So it will basically help you to avoid overplotting but it will also help you to see the distribution because now that the data points are not on top of each other and I'm just counting them and I'm coloring a region based on that. That's all you need as a that's what you need as a viewer of this plot just know the distribution. Using the same strategy, we could go a little bit fancier and instead of just doing the counting or doing the coloring based on the counts, we could do it based on the KVE. So assume that you have all of those points or different indicators for each one of these points in the 2D surface. You put a KDE plot on top of it and you color it based on, for example, the height of those KDE kind of or that KDE function, which essentially is going to tell you that, okay, this is where I have the tallest KDE kind of point or the curve and then as I'm going away into different then the color is going to be lighter blue and then in these areas I have none data point. So that's basically the point that the KDE curve is going to all the way to zero. Right? So this is a 2D KDE version of what we did have before. The way that you can implement it as exactly as what how we would call the KDE plot. Here I would say that I have a KDE plot, but instead of only passing one data point or one column, I would say that I have a data frame and I'm passing you two different columns. So just go ahead and plot it or plot the KDE plot in two dimension using all of these contours that we're seeing.

children.title: Contour Plots, Visualization Techniques, and Importance
children.summary: Contour plots are a 2D representation of Kernel Density Estimation (KDE), similar to topographic maps, where darker colors indicate higher data concentration. Various visualization techniques like scatter plots, hex plots, and density plots are discussed, emphasizing the importance of selecting the right visualization based on data trends. Tools such as seaborn and matplotlib are highlighted for creating these visualizations, with plotly introduced for interactive options. Different types of visualizations are outlined for single and joint distributions, and the significance of visualization in exploratory data analysis (EDA) is stressed, particularly for assessing data readiness, identifying outliers, and communicating results.

children.title: Contour Plots and KDE Visualization
children.summary: Contour plots serve as a 2-dimensional representation of Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) plots, akin to topographic maps where contour lines indicate areas with equal density of data points. Darker colors on the plot signify regions with a higher concentration of data points. The example provided involves plotting adult female literacy rates against per capita growth using the seaborn library's kdeplot function.

children.title: Data Visualization Techniques and Tools
children.summary: The discussion covers various data visualization techniques, including scatter plots, hex plots, and density plots, emphasizing the importance of choosing the right visualization based on data trends rather than individual data points. It mentions the use of seaborn and matplotlib for creating these plots and introduces the plotly framework for more interactive visualizations. The segment encourages students to understand the different plotting functions and their applications while recommending further reading on plotly for future use.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's kind of the stylistic kind of choice that we made. And if you take a look at where we have the darker regions or darker contours, it's exactly corresponding to where we have most of the points. So it's pretty interesting. But notice that if I had the issue of overplotting, I could just stick with the scatterplot, jitter it in place, and just make sense of what exists in the data, or I could go one level advance and then say that I don't care about individual data points, but I want to see the trend. If you want to see the trend, just go with the hexagon kind of plotting in the center or the KDE plot. So that said, all of these plots that we had seen, I don't think that I'll go over any additional kind of plot, except for one. In today's lecture, but all of the plots that we had seen, or you would want to use in data 100 is already covered. What we covered was basically all of these plots that are listed here. We had seen histogram, we had seen density plots, we had seen how we can create density plots using KDE estimation or just put a density curve on top of a histogram, be it's inbox plot, violent plot. But we've also seen all of those kind of, and today we've seen like obviously a scatter plot, hex plot and contrapot. But all of the things that we've seen, we've seen it in seaborn and map plotlet. So you might also see frequently us using good plotlet framework for labs or for lecture, kind of visualization and so on and so forth. but the plot list is going to be a little bit more interactive so we can hover over it and then see different data points. We don't want you to know the syntax of plot list but now that you can you kind of understand what are different types of plotting functions and what's kind of the science behind deciding what function to use in what kind of visualization you use then you can kind of understand plot list as well. But we highly recommend you to go read about it and learn about it and maybe in future version of data 100 we will also incorporate plotly into the core column. It's just that data 100 is still packed that I can't fit in one additional slide into the lectures as you might up guess. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

children.title: Types of Visualization and Tools
children.summary: Visualization requires careful consideration of the type to use. For single variable distributions, options include rug plots, histograms, density plots, box plots, and violin plots. For joint distributions of two quantitative variables, scatter plots, hex plots, and contour plots are appropriate. The course primarily utilizes seaborn and matplotlib for visualizations, while pandas offers basic plotting methods. Additionally, plotly is mentioned as a library for creating interactive plots, which will be used in lectures, labs, and assignments. The next focus will be on the theory behind visualization.

children.title: Importance of Visualization in Data Modeling
children.summary: Visualization plays a crucial role in the exploratory data analysis (EDA) phase, helping to assess if data is ready for modeling, identify outliers, and determine necessary transformations. It aids in communicating results, such as plotting the distribution of model errors. Understanding data trends, including linearity, is essential, as illustrated by plotting Gross National Income (GNI) against adult literacy rates. Visualization helps identify issues like overplotting, indicating data concentration in specific areas, which can affect modeling readiness.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Now we know that okay, if I had one variable what to use, if I had two variables what to use, if I had a specific like... The decision about I want to see the trend versus I want to see a specific data point you will make that decision about the type of the plot accordingly. But the next thing that I want to talk about is about transformation. This is a topic that I said is going to be very important for when we're going into the modeling phase. Kind of going back into why we want to do visualization remember that we wanted to do visualization as part of the EDA. So if you want to make it take a look at your data and then figure out if your data is ready for the modeling phase, if your data that has any outlier or not, if the data needs any transformation or not, these are all the reasons that we might want to use visualization. Additionally, a lot of time we want to communicate results. We'll ask you that, okay, do this modeling and then plot, for example, the distribution of your. The root and the score error of your model so that's something that you want to communicate with us in your homework and whatnot so these are the two reasons that we might want to be we might want to do visualization and a lot of time what we're interested in doing is to figure out if our data is linearized or not and in order to find trends in our data. So for example, what do I mean by that? If you take a look at this specific data set, it plots GNI with respect to adults with literacy rate. why you want to do visualization. children.title: GNI, Literacy, Data Modeling, and Transformations
children.summary: The relationship between Gross National Income (GNI) per capita and adult literacy rates is explored, highlighting that higher GNI correlates with improved literacy, although data interpretation can be complex. Effective visualization methods are crucial for clear data communication. Generalizability in data modeling is discussed, revealing that a vertical data distribution indicates GNI changes have minimal impact on illiteracy rates, necessitating data transformation for linear regression applicability. Linearization techniques, including logarithmic and polynomial transformations, are recommended to enhance model accuracy. The impact of outliers on visualizations is examined, noting that they can distort axis scales in non-linear plots, with log and square root transformations proposed as solutions. These transformations help mitigate the effects of outliers by adjusting large values more than smaller ones. Furthermore, the importance of normalized scales in linear regression is emphasized to improve clarity in visualizing relationships. The segment concludes with a discussion on inverse functions, particularly the exponential function as the inverse of logarithmic functions, and prompts consideration of opposites to square roots and logarithms.

children.title: GNI Impact on Literacy and Data Modeling
children.summary: The relationship between Gross National Income (GNI) per capita and adult literacy rates is examined, noting that as GNI increases, literacy rates tend to improve, though data interpretation can be challenging. The importance of effective visualization methods is highlighted for better data communication. Additionally, the discussion covers generalizability in data modeling, indicating that a vertical data distribution suggests GNI changes do not significantly affect illiteracy rates, thus lacking generalizability. The necessity for transforming data to establish a linear relationship is emphasized, noting that the current data is unsuitable for linear regression without further feature generation. Finally, the segment addresses linearization techniques for regression analysis, advocating for transformations like logarithmic or polynomial adjustments to improve model accuracy.

children.title: Impact of GNI on Adult Literacy Rate
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the relationship between Gross National Income (GNI) per capita and adult literacy rates in various regions. As GNI increases, the trend in adult literacy rates is analyzed, highlighting potential challenges such as data interpretation issues and the difficulty in establishing clear relationships between the variables. The importance of effective visualization methods is emphasized to enhance understanding of data and communication of results.

children.title: Generalizability in Data Modeling
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the importance of generalizability in data modeling, specifically in relation to adult illiteracy rates and Gross National Income (GNI). It explains that if the data distribution appears vertical, it suggests that changes in GNI do not significantly affect the illiteracy rate, indicating a lack of generalizability. The need for a model that accurately captures the trends and characteristics of all data points is emphasized, as well as the necessity of transforming the data to establish a linear relationship between GNI and the transformed adult illiteracy rate. The segment concludes with the assertion that the data is not yet suitable for linear regression without further feature generation and transformation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we don't want the data to be smushed together in one specific area. The other issue that we have is that with this data it's really hard to have a model that's generalizable. We're going to talk about generalizability, I love it in the modeling lectures, but basically what we mean is that if I want to go ahead and model for the trend in this data. Just basically looking at the distribution of this data I would I might for example think that okay the trend in the data is going to be kind of this vertical kind of line that basically says that depending on So that would say that the adult illiteracy rate does not depend on the GNI. So if you increase GNI, for example here, then technically your adult illiteracy rate is not going to change that much because most of your data is on this vertical line. So because of that This model of me thinking that the trend is this vertical line is not going to be generalizable because there is a lot of different data points and the right hand side that I didn't capture or did a model. So we want to have a model that... As accurately as possible captures the trend or the characteristics of all of the data points as much as possible and unabashed has a good accuracy on the entire data set. So because of that we don't necessarily want to have some outliers that are outlying here that we can't necessarily model them. that this data is not ready for being used in a linear regression or any modeling test that we want to have. But instead, what we need to do is we still need to transform the data, do more feature in generating so that the data is ready so that now there is kind of a linear relationship between GNI and also the transform version of GNI and the transform version of the adult will literacy rate. children.title: Linearization of Data for Regression Analysis
children.summary: The focus is on transforming data to linearize the association between two dimensions, which is crucial for linear regression models used in the course. A red linear trend line is introduced to illustrate how it captures the relationship better than an initial vertical line. The importance of linearization is emphasized, as many relationships between variables are best modeled using simple linear regression. The segment discusses methods for achieving linearization, including specific transformations like logarithmic or polynomial adjustments, and encourages students to experiment with these transformations in their homework to address various plotting issues.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Stop! What we want is exactly going from left hand side to the right hand side. What you're seeing is that the scales on both of the axes are now different. Now there is a general linear trend that is captured using that red linear line. And then you can see that generally this captures the trend more or less better than that vertical line that I had initially or I thought about initially. Okay? So what we're essentially doing in data 100 when we talk about you need to do transformation is that we're trying to linearize the data, linearize the association between. One dimension versus another dimension. Why do we care about linearization and not any other kind of transformation? For one is that we can do this transform versions could be used in a linear regression model, which is the first model that you're seeing in data 100. Another reason is that a lot of time when you want to understand the relationship between two variables, it is modeled best with a simple linear regression explanation. children.title: Impact of Outliers and Data Transformation Techniques
children.summary: Outliers can significantly distort visualizations in non-linear plots by affecting axis scales, with large outlying x values distorting the horizontal axis and many large y values compressing the vertical axis. To address these distortions, transformations such as log and square root can be applied. Log transformation reduces large values while affecting smaller values less, bringing outliers closer to the origin, while square root transformation similarly impacts large values more than smaller ones. Additionally, data clumping in linear regression can hinder model performance; having a normalized scale between axes is crucial to avoid difficulty in distinguishing clustered data points, which reduces clarity in visualizing relationships.

children.title: Impact of Outliers on Non-linear Plots
children.summary: Outliers can significantly distort visualizations by affecting axis scales. Specifically, large outlying x values can distort the horizontal axis, while many large y values can compress the vertical axis, leading to non-linear representations in plots.

children.title: Impact of Data Clumping on Linear Regression
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the issue of data points being clustered together in a plot, particularly in relation to linear regression. It highlights the importance of having a normalized scale between the x-axis and y-axis for optimal model performance. The speaker explains that when multiple y-values correspond to the same x-value, it becomes difficult to distinguish between them, which can hinder the interpretation of the data. The goal is to avoid having data points 'smushed' together, as this reduces clarity in visualizing relationships and differences in the dataset.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But what is the issue with this specific region? That all of these values are clumped together in the top left corner. Can someone yes? What is difficult about it? So... There is one thing that the scale between x-axis and y-axis are not the same. That's the separate issue. Yes, so that the scale thing we're going to come back to that in the linear regression lectures that you really need to make sure that our dimensions are always normalized for the best performance of to get the best performance of a linear regression model. Did you want to add something? So if I want to fit the best fit of the data basically here, it essentially says that For the same value of x if the data points are really kind of clumped together in this vertical axis. For the same x value, I have multiple different y values and those y values are different, right? And basically, I want to see a distinction between them so that as I change x value or as I keep the same x value, I can see the distinction between them. I don't want to have multiple different values really smushed together at the top for the same x or for different values of x. Even for example, if you take a look at, for example, this point right here and this point right here, The x values are entirely different, but then the y values are exactly the same. If you could make a difference between them so that one of them is set at the top of the other one, then you could see a difference between them better. That, okay, when x is different, then there is this light difference in y, so things are going to make more sense. children.title: Data Transformation Techniques: Log and Square Root
children.summary: The process of addressing outlier issues in data distributions involves transforming the X dimension to achieve a more uniform spread. Two common transformations are log transformation and square root transformation. Log transformation significantly reduces large values while proportionally affecting smaller values less, bringing outliers closer to the origin and aligning them more closely with the rest of the data. For example, transforming data points from the 70,000 range to around 12 illustrates this effect. Similarly, square root transformation also impacts large values more than smaller ones, providing another method to adjust the scale and distribution of data points.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we need to fix those of these issues. But I'm going to basically try to spell out what's the process of fixing each one of those issues. How can I fix this issue of having all of these data points be kind of outlier in the distribution in the right hand side? How can I make sure that my X dimension is kind of scaled in a way that these data points are going to come closer to the rest of the data points? And then basically now I have the transform version of the X dimension to be kind of more uniformly spread out in the X axis as opposed to some of them kind of here and then very few in the outlier in the right hand side. The kind of transformation that we can do is we can use log transformation or we can use the square root transformation. So with the log transformation, if you have a very large value and you take the log of that, it will substantially decrease that value. But if you have a small value and you take a log, it will get a smaller but proportionally less the smaller. So the delta of all of these outlier points for coming closer to the origin is going to be more than how much these smaller values are getting closer to the origin. So that's why the distribution is going to get a little bit closer to each other. And then what we're going to see is that, okay, all of these data points, now instead of being, for example, in the 70,000 range, they are in the 12 range and they're closer to each other, right? And they're closer to also the rest of the values that that we have here. All of the data points are going to be transformed and to a smaller version of themselves. That's how the log works. But then it will fix the scale that we have. Any kind of transformation that will impact large values different than the small values and it will proportionally decrease the large values more than the small values would work here, such as a score root, right? So if you have, for example, a score root of 1600 versus a square root of 16. This is going to be, for example, 4. This is going to be 40, right? So both of them are going to get a smaller, but proportionally 1600 got way smaller than 16 got a smaller. So that's why they're going to get closer to each other. children.title: Transformations for Non-linear Data Visualization
children.summary: Extreme values in data can distort visualizations, particularly on the x-axis. Log transformations can help address this by compressing large values, making the distribution more uniform. However, when data points are clustered together at higher ranges, further adjustments are needed to separate them. The discussion emphasizes the need for transformations that can push large values further apart, improving visibility and interpretation of the data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Every time that we have extreme values, any of such transformations such as log is scored with things like that would work and will help us to have a uniform kind of distribution in the x-axis or in the bi-axis. So one problem solved. Now our X dimension is log of the G and I right by the X axis is still the same. And you can see that the X-axis is still has this issue of all of these data points kind of clumped together at the higher range that we still need to fix. Okay, going into fixing that. What kind of transformation can I use so that I can push those large values more upward so that I can see the difference between them is slightly better. This is the opposite problem. In the first case, I had the issue that the data points were far apart. I needed to bring them closer. Here the data points are on top of each other. I just need to push them to be farther away from each other. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

children.title: Inverse Functions and Data Transformations
children.summary: The segment discusses inverse functions, specifically identifying the exponential function as the inverse of logarithmic functions. It prompts consideration of opposites to square roots and logarithms. The application of transformations to non-linear data is explored, emphasizing how raising values to a power can clarify data visualization, particularly when large y values cluster together. An example involving adult literacy rates plotted against the log of gross national income per capita illustrates the impact of these transformations on data interpretation. Additionally, power transformations are highlighted for their effectiveness in distinguishing small differences in non-linear plots.

children.title: Inverse Functions: Logarithm and Power
children.summary: The discussion revolves around the concept of inverse functions, particularly focusing on the inverse of logarithmic functions, which is the exponential function. The speaker prompts the audience to think about what would be opposite to a square root or logarithm, leading to the conclusion that the exponential function serves as the inverse. An example is hinted at, although not fully elaborated.

children.title: Power Transformations in Data Visualization
children.summary: Raising input values to a power can help distinguish small differences in data, particularly in non-linear plots. When analyzing a plot where large y values are clumped together, applying a power transformation to the y data can resolve this issue. Specifically, raising large numbers to a power significantly increases their value, while small numbers remain relatively unchanged. This technique enhances the visualization of relationships in the data, particularly when examining adult literacy rates against gross national income per capita.

children.title: Applying Log and Power Transformations for Linear Regression
children.summary: Two transformations were applied to improve the analysis of data: a log transformation of the x-axis to reduce outlier effects and a power transformation (raising y to the power of 4) to spread the data points. This resulted in a semi-linear trend, allowing for the application of a linear regression model. The new dimensions for the regression are log of x for the x-axis and y to the power of 4 for the y-axis, enabling the use of the linear regression equation y = mx + b to model the relationship.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So you can see that now all of those data points are going to be more spreaded out. Now I can see the trend better. So I did two transformations to solve two problems. I first did a log transformation or a transformation that would help me reduce the range of the x-axis so I don't have an outlier. And then I also raised my y-axis to the power of 4. I could do e to the power of those numbers, any exponential kind of function with a work here. But if I do that, essentially what you're seeing is that now my data the plants are spread a little bit more. And now what we're seeing is a semi-linear trend as opposed to that non-linear trend that we had seen before. So what we can do with this is now put a linear regression line. Now we can understand the relationship between G and I and adulculatory serrate using a linear regression model. What do I mean by that? So if I have a linear regression line from data aid, you can see that I have X dimension, I have Y dimension, it just happens that my X dimension is now log of X. And then I have Y dimension, that my Y dimension just happens to be now. y to the power of 4 or that variable to the power of 4, which essentially will help me to use the linear regression line based on the template that we have that was y equal to m times x plus b. So x right now is log of x. y right now is y to the power of 4. So now I have a linear regression line that models the relationship between log of x and y to the power of 4. children.title: Importance of Data Transformation in Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses the significance of data transformation in revealing relationships between variables that may not be apparent from raw data. It explains how transforming the equation to a linear form, such as y = m log(x) + b, allows for a clearer understanding and plotting of the relationship between the variables. The speaker emphasizes that proper data preparation and transformation enhance the power of modeling, leading to more accurate insights from linear regression, and highlights the ability to extract trends from transformed data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: which is pretty neat. We couldn't do it or we couldn't have guessed this kind of association by just looking at the plot. That's why we needed to do transformation and that's why it's pretty important. So what we can do afterwards is to just restructure this equation and just write the equation as y equal to the transform version of the right hand side. So I can say that, okay, m times log of x plus b to the power of 1 over 4 is the equation that you're looking for, which I can go ahead and plot that. We would have never guessed that this is the relationship between between these two variables, but now using linear regression, we fit it in non-linear function or association between the x dimension and the y dimension. Now you see that I'm living in the xy dimension, because I can because I have the equation for it. It's pretty neat. This is what I want you to take away from this lecture that when we teach you linear regression, we teach you way more than linear regression, we teach you techniques that if you do... If you prepare your data data well enough and you do the transformation good enough and you have all of the features in kind of linear space and also the same scale from each other. The modeling that you will do is going to be very more powerful if you transform it back to the original kind of space of your data. And this is the kind of trend that we will be able to extract using a simple linear regression. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

children.title: Using Tukey-Masa Diagram for Data Transformation
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of identifying and applying transformations to data based on its distribution, specifically using the Tukey-Masa or Balch diagram as a reference. It emphasizes the importance of reasoning about data bulges and determining whether to transform the X-axis, Y-axis, or both, depending on the shape of the data. The speaker illustrates this by referencing a specific trend observed in the data and explains that sometimes only one transformation may be necessary to achieve the desired results.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Mother. Okay, you might tell me that Nargis, this was difficult to just guess what issues we have and how to fix those issues. And it was kind of a guessing game that you use while you're to the power for a lot of X for fixing each one of those issues. But I think that was a good practice for you to see how I arrived at each one of those transformations that I did. For future, what you can do is you can start to reason in your mind in the same way that I started reasoning about the plot that we'd see, right? But additionally, you can use this chart. This is called Tukimasa or Balch diagram as kind of your reference guide. Essentially what you would do is you would take a look at where, how your data bulges. And if your data bulges in a certain way, you would take a look at the transformation and the x-axis and the y-axis and those are the transformations that you could use. Sometimes only a single transformation might solve your problem, so you might only want to transform X, or you might only want to transform Y. In some cases, you might want to transform both in order to get the result that you want. So kind of to put it in practice, the trend that we saw in our data was that our data was bulging in the direction that we're seeing in this top left corner. And what we did was we basically kind of guess what kind of transformation I need on the X axis, what kind of transformation I need. I need a new y-axis and we're going to head and transform the data. Again, I did both of the transformation. You might not need both. You might only need one depending on the shape of your data and depending on how deep it bulges into one direction. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza X

children.title: Transformations in Data Analysis
children.summary: Discusses various transformations for data analysis, including log transformations, square root transformations, and power transformations (e.g., y to the power of two or three). Examples include applying these transformations to data such as adult literacy rates and gross national income per capita, illustrating how different transformations can be used effectively.

children.title: Tukey-Mosteller Bulge Diagram and Data Transformations
children.summary: The Tukey-Mosteller Bulge Diagram is introduced as a reference tool that will be provided on the reference sheet, emphasizing the importance of understanding the reasoning behind it rather than memorizing the plot. The discussion includes potential transformations for data, such as transforming the dependent variable (y) using methods like y^2 or y^3, and transforming the independent variable (x) through options like log(x) or sqrt(x). The segment also touches on the broader context of plotting distributions, relationships between quantitative variables, and visualization theory.

children.title: Visualization Theory and Data Preparation
children.summary: The discussion emphasizes the importance of balancing data readiness for modeling with the need for further data preparation and processing. It introduces visualization theory, covering topics such as plotting distributions, relationships between quantitative variables, and various harnessing techniques including X/Y axes, color, markings, conditioning, and context.

children.title: Effective Plotting Considerations for Data Visualization
children.summary: Different considerations for creating meaningful plots are discussed, emphasizing the importance of designing visualizations for human interpretation. An example is provided using Titanic ticket prices plotted against age, highlighting that the majority of data is overplotted, making it difficult to extract useful information. The segment suggests that visualizations should convey clear insights, indicating that older individuals did not spend more on tickets compared to younger ones.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Um, this is kind of going to be a little bit of... a little bit eye opening in a sense that you will see different considerations that you should have when you're creating different plots. There's not going to be any code involved but it's going to be interesting for you to see how you can effectively use different information channels. Why are we talking about information channel because we need to make sure that the plots that you're creating that you're creating actually for humans are meaningful. So for example, here what I plotted is the amount of the amount that people paid for, the ticket for Titanic and depending on their age. And if you're taking a look at what people are going to see in this plot, people are not going to be able to gather much information from it, because majority of your data is kind of overplotted in the bottom area here. So you can make that okay, how much a 20 year old person is gonna pay versus a 60 year old necessarily because the data is just spread it out so in the zero to hundred dollar range. children.title: Importance of Visualization in Data Analysis
children.summary: Effective use of information channels is crucial, particularly in understanding the benefits of visualization. Different datasets can have identical summary statistics, such as means and standard deviations, yet their visual representations can reveal significant differences. Visualization complements summary statistics by providing insights into model performance across various data ranges. When reporting model accuracy, it is essential to include both the evaluation metric and a plot of the distribution to capture comprehensive information about the model's performance. A discussion on the effectiveness of different visualization types, such as histograms or KDEs compared to scatter plots, highlights the expressiveness of visualizations over summary statistics.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So what we need to make sure is that we use information channels more effectively. And what we're gonna do is that we will also try to understand why visualization in the first place is helpful. So What do you what I plot it here is a set of A set of different data points that have the same summary statistic. So you're seeing that the mean of the x-axis, the mean of the y-axis, standard deviations, all of that is the same. But when you visualize data sets, they're very different from each other. So that's why we think about summary statistic and visualization always being like really good complementary things that would go together. Every time that we want you to report the accuracy of your model or the output of your model, we would tell you that, okay, give us the metric or the value for the metric of the evaluation, as well as plot the distribution so that we can kind of see how your model is performing for different range of the data, kind of to make sure that we capture all the information that we can get about your model performance. So mean by information channel? So let's take a look at this exercise. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

children.title: Visualizing Age Data and Encoding Strategies
children.summary: The segment covers visualizing the age of Titanic passengers using various plot types, highlighting the importance of human visual perception in data representation. It discusses methods like histograms, KDE, and the rock plot created with SMS.rock blood, which displays individual ages but may encounter overplotting issues. The Y-axis represents data points based on specific age values. The use of 2D markers for one-dimensional data in two-dimensional plots is explored, focusing on effective encoding of multiple variables through channels such as color, area, shapes, and shading to enhance data visualization. Additionally, it addresses the misuse of length in visualizations, particularly in bar charts of car nationalities, which can mislead viewers regarding rankings. Alternative methods like scatter plots are recommended to prevent false ordering. The segment concludes with a discussion on data transformation to improve visualization interpretability, emphasizing linearizing data for better model effectiveness, with an example involving adult literacy rates and gross national income per capita.

children.title: Visualizing Age Data and Encoding Strategies
children.summary: The segment discusses visualizing the age of Titanic passengers using various plot types, emphasizing the importance of human visual perception in data representation. It covers methods like histograms, KDE, and the rock plot created with SMS.rock blood, which shows individual ages but may face overplotting issues. The Y-axis represents data points based on specific age values. Additionally, the use of 2D markers is explored for representing one-dimensional data in two-dimensional plots, focusing on effective encoding of multiple variables through various information channels such as color, area, shapes, and shading, which can enhance data visualization.

children.title: Visualizing Age Data from Titanic Passengers
children.summary: A discussion on visualizing the age of Titanic passengers using various plot types. The segment emphasizes the importance of leveraging human visual perception in data representation and begins to explore basic plot types suitable for displaying age data.
