children.title: Plotting Age Distribution with Rock Plot
children.summary: Different methods for visualizing data are discussed, including histograms, KDE, and the rock plot, which is created using the command SMS.rock blood. The rock plot displays individual data points, indicating the age of individuals from infants to 72 years old, although it may suffer from overplotting. The Y-axis is used to represent the data, with markers placed according to specific age values. The process of creating the plot involves encoding the data into the Y-axis position, allowing for customization of markers and their encoding.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: There are different ways that I can plot it. There is a way that I can directly use histogram, we have seen it before, I can also use KDE, or I can use rock plot. This is called the rock blood. The way that you can call rock blood is just SMS.rock blood. It's not going to be a very fun blood, but what it does is basically that at every given data point, it will put a marker. So essentially, what you're seeing here is that it is telling me that the age of the individual, there are some people who were like infants and we have indicators for them. We have, for example, a person that looks like that they were kind of in 72 year old and we basically tried to have an indicator for each month of them. As you can see for trying to plot this distribution or look at the age distribution of individuals, there will be a lot of overplodding, right? So this is not going to be a really good visualization, but just so you know that we used exactly one channel in this exact example, that that channel is basically the Y axis. So I'm using the Y access in order to plot this information. So what I can do next is I can go ahead and say and show you how we're creating the plot using the channels that we have. So I have the data frame or I have that column specific leap. I would say that I'm using a marker for each one of these individual rows. So I'm saying that the marker that I'm going to use for the rows is just this horizontal line. And then I'm using a specific encoding. for the channel that I want to use. What is the channel? The channel is the y-axis. So I would say that, okay, for this is specific age of 22, put it in the pixel 10 position from the bottom. So it will go ahead and put not pixel 10. Pixel 10 would be somewhere here on 22. And it will basically take one marker, encoded into location, and then that's how the rot plot is actually created. We could go ahead and change how we're using the marker or the encoding. For example, if I change the marker to be like these circles, what I can create is what you're seeing on the right hand side. So the position is still the same. The encoding is the same, but the marker is different. So you can see that the choice of marker here is also important, because I was able to tell the differences between different ages using the normal rock plot, but with the marker being different, now I can't basically see anything because I'm plotting it, I'm using it to the marker in a 1D encoding, if that makes sense. children.title: 2D Markers and Information Channels in Plots
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the use of 2D markers to represent one-dimensional information in a two-dimensional plot, such as a scatter plot showing the relationship between age and fear. It emphasizes the importance of utilizing multiple information channels effectively, questioning how many variables are encoded in the plot and inviting explanations about the channels used.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Right, because it basically grabs the y-axis, x-axis, and also it has the width, it grabs also the y-axis. So it has two dimensions in order to encode only one dimension information that we have. So the more appropriate place to use this specific 2D marker for us would have been a 2D plot or a plot that has a 2-dimensional encoding. So a two dimensional encoding could be that, okay, I want to do a scatter plot. I want to take a look at the association between age and fear. So in that case, a 2D marker would have been helpful because I can say that, okay, I'm gonna use these two dimensions of the age being 22 and the fair being 7.25. And I'm gonna encode it in two dimensional space as opposed to one dimensional space. And in that case, it will make sense for me to have a 2D marker in a 2D channel space. So this is what we're going to start talking about and thinking about about how many information channels are being utilized and how to effectively use all the information channels that we have. So for example, in this example, we encoded more than three variables. But what I want to ask you is that how many variables are being coding here exactly? So the question was how many channels are being used or how many variables are being recording in this plot. Looks like Ford is a winner. Can someone explain what those Ford channels are? leaf_chunk.audio: Right, because it basically grabs the y-axis, x-axis, and also it has the width, it grabs also the y-axis. So it has two dimensions in order to encode only one dimension information that we have. So the more appropriate place to use this specific 2D marker for us would have been a 2D plot or a plot that has a 2-dimensional encoding. So a two dimensional encoding could be that, okay, I want to do a scatter plot. I want to take a look at the association between age and fear. So in that case, a 2D marker would have been helpful because I can say that, okay, I'm gonna use these two dimensions of the age being 22 and the fair being 7.25. And I'm gonna encode it in two dimensional space as opposed to one dimensional space. And in that case, it will make sense for me to have a 2D marker in a 2D channel space. So this is what we're going to start talking about and thinking about about how many information channels are being utilized and how to effectively use all the information channels that we have. So for example, in this example, we encoded more than three variables. But what I want to ask you is that how many variables are being coding here exactly? So the question was how many channels are being used or how many variables are being recording in this plot. Looks like Ford is a winner. Can someone explain what those Ford channels are?

children.title: Strategies for Encoding Multiple Variables
children.summary: The discussion focuses on utilizing four different information channels for effective data visualization, emphasizing the importance of strategizing which channel to use based on the variables being represented. It highlights the various encodings available, such as color and area, and suggests that additional channels like shapes and shading can further enhance the representation, leading to infinite possibilities in data encoding.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Yes. X and Y 2. We have the color tree. We have the area of each of those circles that we have. So we have four different information channels that we're utilizing. And this is how we're basically trying to strategize what information channel to use, how to use that information channel, and then what's the priority in terms of which channel is more appropriate for what kind of variable that we need to create. the rest of the lecture is that if you're thinking about all of those channels and all of those encodings that we can have, how to effectively use it. children.title: Misuse of Length and Encoding in Visualizations
children.summary: The segment discusses the improper use of length in data visualizations, particularly in bar charts depicting car nationalities. It emphasizes how the length of bars can mislead viewers into believing there is a ranking or superiority among cars based on nationality, which is inaccurate. Alternative visualization methods, such as scatter plots, are suggested to avoid implying false ordering. The misuse of length encoding is further illustrated with an example showing how it misrepresents the data by suggesting that cars from the USA are superior in length or quality. The lecture will continue with a focus on best practices for various encoding methods after a brief break.

children.title: Misuse of Length in Data Visualization
children.summary: The segment discusses the improper use of length in bar charts, specifically in visualizing car nationalities. It highlights how the length of the bars can mislead viewers into thinking there is a ranking or superiority among the cars based on their nationality, which is inaccurate. The discussion emphasizes the need for alternative visualization methods, such as scatter plots, that do not imply false ordering or comparison among the data points.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I'm going to show you this plot basically showing you the worst example of when the x-axis or the y-axis could be utilized. So in this example, what you're seeing is that the x-axis or the length same formation of the x-axis is being abused. In a sense that when we're trying to plot the association of different cars to different nationalities or to different countries, what we're seeing here is that if USA is the first, that means that there is something less about a car because the length of the bar is smaller as opposed to basically having a scatter point for all of these dimensions that we have or any other kind of plots that we could use. Because the length of these bars here does not have does not. In code any information and the ordering of all of the data points that we have here and also the data points that we have here does not include any information. children.title: Abusing Encodings in Visualizations
children.summary: This segment discusses the misuse of the length encoding in visualizations, highlighting an example where a bar chart incorrectly suggests an ordering of car lengths based on nationality. The example illustrates how the lengths of the bars imply that cars from the USA are superior in length or quality compared to those from other nations, which misrepresents the data. The lecture will further explore appropriate uses of various aspects of visualizations.

[ ]:
# Some data cleaning to help with the next example
df = pd.DataFrame(index=wb.index)
df['lit'] = wb['Adult literacy rate: Female: % ages 15 and older: 2005-14'] \
+ wb["Adult literacy rate: Male: % ages 15 and older: 2005-14"]
df['inc' ] = wb['gni']
df.dropna(inplace=True)
plt.scatter(df["inc"], df[ "lit"])
plt.xlabel("Gross national income per capita")
plt.ylabel("Adult literacy rate");
What is making this plot non-linear?
. There are a few extremely large values for gross national income that are distorting the horizontal scale of the plot. If we rescaled the x-values
such that these large values become proportionally smaller, the plot would be more linear
. There are too many large values of adult literacy rate all clumped together at the top of the plot. If we rescaled the y-axis such that large values of
y are more spread out, the plot would be more linear
First, we can transform the x-values such that very large values of x become smaller. This can be achieved by performing a log transformation of the
gross national income data. When we take the logarithm of a large number, this number becomes proportionally much smaller relative to its original
value. When we take the log of a small number, the number does not change very significantly relative to its starting value.
[ ]:
# np. Log compute the natural (base e) Logarithm
plt.scatter(np.log(df["inc"]), df[ "lit"])

children.title: Data Transformation for Visualization Improvement
children.summary: Transforming data can enhance the interpretability of visualizations, especially in understanding linear relationships through slopes and intercepts. The course will focus on linearizing data to improve model effectiveness. An example discussed involves cleaning data by combining adult literacy rates for males and females and plotting it against gross national income per capita, which initially appears non-linear due to extreme values. A log transformation of the income data is suggested to proportionally reduce large values and improve linearity. Additionally, considerations for creating effective plots include the efficient use of scale, color, marking, conditioning, and context, with emphasis on how transformations can clarify visualization interpretation.

children.title: Transforming Data for Better Visualizations
children.summary: Transforming data can enhance the interpretability of visualizations, particularly in understanding linear relationships through slopes and intercepts. The course will later focus on linearizing data to improve model effectiveness. An example involves cleaning data by combining adult literacy rates for males and females and plotting it against gross national income per capita. The plot is initially non-linear due to extreme values in gross national income and a clustering of high literacy rates. To address this, a log transformation of the income data is suggested, which proportionally reduces large values and improves the linearity of the plot.

children.title: Creating Effective Plots: Scale and Transformation
children.summary: Considerations for creating plots include efficient use of scale, color, marking, conditioning, and context. Transforming data can enhance visualization interpretation, particularly through linear relationships which clarify slopes and intercepts. An example involves preparing data on adult literacy rates and gross national income, highlighting issues with non-linear plots due to extreme values. Rescaling x-values via log transformation can help normalize data and improve plot linearity.

Audio: If you're thinking about lengths or these bars measuring any quantitative value. So again, what I said was we're going to take a look at different considerations when you're creating plots. children.title: Analysis of Planned Parenthood Hearing and Data Visualization Techniques
children.summary: The segment analyzes the 2015 congressional hearing involving Planned Parenthood, focusing on Congressman Chaffetz's graph that shows a decrease in cancer screenings and an increase in abortions from 2006 to 2013. It discusses the implications of the data and the messaging of the graph, raising questions about its plotting. The segment then covers data visualization techniques, emphasizing the importance of using a single scale for both datasets to avoid misleading interpretations and advocating for percentage visualizations. It highlights the need for consistent Y-axis scales and side-by-side comparisons for clarity. Additionally, it reviews color maps in data visualization, demonstrating their impact on accessibility for color-blind individuals and discussing the use of perceptually uniform color maps to improve data interpretation.

children.title: Analysis of Planned Parenthood Hearing and Graph
children.summary: The segment discusses the 2015 congressional hearing involving Planned Parenthood, which faced accusations of selling aborted fetal tissue for profit. It highlights Congressman Chaffetz's presentation, which included a graph showing a decrease in cancer screenings and an increase in abortions from 2006 to 2013. The analysis focuses on the implications of the data, the messaging of the graph, and questions regarding its plotting and any potentially suspicious elements in its presentation.

children.title: Case Study on Planned Parenthood Hearing
children.summary: In 2015, Planned Parenthood faced accusations of selling aborted fetal tissue for profit, leading to a congressional hearing. The discussion highlighted a plot presented by Congressman Chaffetz, which illustrated a decrease in cancer screenings and an increase in abortions from 2006 to 2013. The analysis aimed to investigate the implications of this data and the messaging conveyed by the graph.

children.title: Data Visualization Techniques and Comparisons
children.summary: The discussion covers data visualization methods in the context of cancer screening and abortion statistics, noting a trend where decreased cancer screenings correlate with increased abortions. Emphasis is placed on using a single scale for both datasets to prevent misleading interpretations, suggesting visualization as percentages of total cases for clarity. The importance of side-by-side comparisons for specific years, like 2006 and 2013, is highlighted, advocating for consistent Y-axis scales and the use of percentage changes instead of raw counts. Additionally, techniques for visualizing COVID case data in Australia are analyzed, recommending axis truncation to highlight significant spikes effectively.

children.title: Data Visualization in Cancer Screening and Abortions
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the comparison of cancer screening and abortion statistics, highlighting a trend where a decrease in cancer screenings corresponds with an increase in abortions. The importance of using a single scale for both datasets is emphasized to avoid misleading interpretations. It is suggested that visualizing the data as percentages of total cases would provide a clearer comparison, ensuring that both sets of data are accurately represented on the same scale.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: In a sense that here we're plotting 2 million, the value of 2 million and the cancerous screening is starting at the top and then going down to almost a million. But when you're looking at the other plots, so you're seeing that the abortion numbers are starting from upper 2000 number and then to lower 300,000 cases. But you're seeing that almost the endpoint of the number of abortions and this starting point of the cancer looks to be at the same place that you might think that one went down and one exactly went up and that's how they're going to be compromised. That's how cancer screening and abortions are basically trying to be equal. the total across one specific year is going to be equal. That's why the decrease in one is equal to the increase in the other one. But the issue is that they're on different scales. The better way for us to visualize this data so that it's not kind of communicating, misleading information, because at first sight you might have that misleading information is to put one scale. This is very, very important. So there is one scale, specifically, that that better data scale is just the percentage of the cases or the total number of cases. So you basically compare magnitudes on the same scale exactly. And you don't have like an imaginary scale for one line, an imaginary scale for another line and just compare and contrast them. children.title: Comparing Cancer Screenings and Abortions
children.summary: The discussion focuses on comparing cancer screenings and abortion cases, highlighting that despite an increase in abortions, cancer screenings remain more prevalent. It emphasizes the importance of visualizing data effectively, suggesting the use of side-by-side comparisons for specific years, such as 2006 and 2013, with distinct colors to enhance clarity. The Y-axis scale should remain consistent to accurately reflect changes, and plotting percentage changes rather than raw counts can provide a clearer understanding of trends.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So using the top one, you can see that, okay, the cancer screening went down for whatever reason and abortion went up, but still proportionally there is way more cancer screenings than abortion cases. You could also do percentages. So this is an example that the percentage is also shown. So you can kind of see that cancer is screening when down proportionally. And then you can see that abortion cases went up and then you can kind of compare the percentages with each other. And then looking at those percentages, again, is going to give you some information about one is increasing and one is decreasing and so on and so forth. So the better a better way to visualize exactly the same information is to just compare exactly what you want to, the things that you want to compare. What you want to compare is exactly the years. What happens in year 2006 and what happens in the year 2013. And what you want is to have different colors for them and have side-by-side comparisons between them. Again, the Y-axis is still the same. The scale is the same between them. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

children.title: Importance of Scale in Data Visualization
children.summary: When comparing categories such as abortion and cancer screenings, it's crucial to consider the scale of the data to ensure accurate representation. For instance, while abortion cases increased from 13% to 26% of total procedures between 2006 and 2013, cancer screenings are proportionally more prevalent. This highlights the need for shared scales in visualizations to avoid misinterpretation and to effectively convey the relationship between similar data sets.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But again, you can look at the category of abortion, the category of cancer. You would see that there is a decline. You would see that there is an increase. But also, you would see that proportionally, there is way more cancer is screen than abortion cases. So this is kind of seeing all that information in one place. So this is kind of making sure that the scales are the same. The scales are shared. The scales are not abused in the case of car nationalities that we had seen before. And that's a very important information. Also speaking of the scale, you don't necessarily need to show and reveal the entire data. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

children.title: Data Visualization Techniques for COVID Cases
children.summary: An analysis of COVID case data in Australia from March 2020 to August 2021 highlights the importance of focusing on specific data areas when visualizing trends. It is recommended to truncate axes to emphasize significant spikes, as demonstrated by comparing two plots. The right-hand plot is more effective for studying spikes, while the left is better for general trends. Additionally, maximizing the use of plot space enhances resolution, and it is acceptable to start the y-axis at a value above zero to improve clarity and focus on relevant data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is an example of I think the COVID cases in Australia and what you're seeing that it starts from March 4 or sometime in March 2020 and then it goes all the way to August of 2021. And the data is very spreaded out. You would see those as small spikes here and there, but then you would see that largest spike happening around meat March, right? If you want to explain what's happening when you had a very kind of high peak, you'd really want to make sure that you only show the area that is of interest. It doesn't, you don't need to show the entire data, but truncate your data or truncate the x-axis or y-axis to focus on exactly what you're interested in. So this is also another important information that the right hand side plot is much better than the left hand side plot if your interests or your use case is to study this spike. If your interest is just to look at the trend generally, yes, should the entire data and use that entire data. What you need to also make sure is that you use the entire kind of canvas of the plot as much as possible. Why do I say it? Because what you're seeing is that The markings here are far apart from each other so each one of them is 400,000 data points or data points apart from Is that so that's 40,000 40,000 data points apart from each other right if you want to have a more resolution what you could do is you could basically use up this extra Wife space that we have and zoom in to only look at the differences in the area of the plot or your line that you're interested in so that's a very important information. The other important information that is done properly on this plot is that between 0 and 20 or 2.1K, the data is not shown. Which is reasonable you don't need to always start from the origin you can basically troncate and say that I'm starting from this minimum value that is on my plot but then go all the way to kind of around the area of the maximum value in your plot so that you get the maximum resolution for the area that you have the changes in your plot so that's something else to consider. leaf_chunk.audio: So this is an example of I think the COVID cases in Australia and what you're seeing that it starts from March 4 or sometime in March 2020 and then it goes all the way to August of 2021. And the data is very spreaded out. You would see those as small spikes here and there, but then you would see that largest spike happening around meat March, right? If you want to explain what's happening when you had a very kind of high peak, you'd really want to make sure that you only show the area that is of interest. It doesn't, you don't need to show the entire data, but truncate your data or truncate the x-axis or y-axis to focus on exactly what you're interested in. So this is also another important information that the right hand side plot is much better than the left hand side plot if your interests or your use case is to study this spike. If your interest is just to look at the trend generally, yes, should the entire data and use that entire data. What you need to also make sure is that you use the entire kind of canvas of the plot as much as possible. Why do I say it? Because what you're seeing is that The markings here are far apart from each other so each one of them is 400,000 data points or data points apart from Is that so that's 40,000 40,000 data points apart from each other right if you want to have a more resolution what you could do is you could basically use up this extra Wife space that we have and zoom in to only look at the differences in the area of the plot or your line that you're interested in so that's a very important information. The other important information that is done properly on this plot is that between 0 and 20 or 2.1K, the data is not shown. Which is reasonable you don't need to always start from the origin you can basically troncate and say that I'm starting from this minimum value that is on my plot but then go all the way to kind of around the area of the maximum value in your plot so that you get the maximum resolution for the area that you have the changes in your plot so that's something else to consider.

children.title: Importance and Types of Color Maps in Visualization
children.summary: The significance of color in data visualization is highlighted, particularly regarding accessibility for color-blind individuals. A demonstration using Color Oracle software illustrates how various color maps impact data visibility for different color deficiencies. The segment contrasts the jet color map with others, showing how color choice affects data interpretation. It also discusses the influence of color maps on X-ray interpretation, noting that while radiologists can interpret details, general viewers may be misled by bright colors. The concept of perceptually uniform color maps is introduced to ensure consistent lightness distribution. Different types of color maps are outlined, including qualitative, sequential, and divergent maps, with examples such as plasma, inferno, and magma provided, emphasizing the importance of selecting appropriate color maps for clarity and accuracy.

children.title: Importance of Color in Data Visualization
children.summary: The significance of color in data visualization is discussed, particularly in relation to accessibility for individuals with color blindness. A demonstration using the Color Oracle software shows how different color maps affect the visibility of data for people with various types of color deficiencies. The segment emphasizes the importance of choosing effective color maps, as different colors encode different intensities or values in plots. An example contrasts the jet color map with another, highlighting how central areas are represented differently, demonstrating the impact of color choice on data interpretation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Thinking about the color. The color is really important. And I'm going to talk a little bit about why it's important and what's the science behind choosing different colors. So this is an example that for people who are not red-green blind and they have full vision, they would see a number inside this figure. But if you go ahead and simulate it, let me see. If I can get the simulation going. Good. So if we go ahead and simulate that you can also simulate it using different kind of software that you have, but what I'm using is color oracle, which basically it will simulate what people with different kinds of color blindness would experience if they look at the plot that you created or they look at the slides that you create. A lot of time when we create a slides, we also simulate it to make sure that all the figures are visible by all the students as well. So for example, if I go ahead and simulate this view, You could kind of guess that there is some number in the center but it's really hard to see. And again, you can simulate for different types of color deficiencies. And then you would basically be able to see if your plot is accessible or not. So speaking of accessibility, what makes a plot accessible or useful or choice of the color More effective than other kind of color choices is based on the color maps that we're choosing So each kind of color map has a set of different colors and different colors are going to encode different intensities or different values on your plot So for example here The same concept or same set of pixel values are shown or visualized by jet color map and also wear this color map. So you can see that there are like central pieces that are highlighted using does. Here it would be. the red area and here it would be the yellow area and there are other areas that are less active around the corner and those are all the blue areas that you see in both color maps. So what I'm going to talk a little bit about is why jet color map is not useful and what can go wrong with the color map. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

children.title: Impact of Color Maps in X-ray Interpretation
children.summary: The discussion focuses on how color maps, particularly the jet color map, affect the interpretation of X-ray images. It highlights that while radiologists can discern important details despite distractions from color, general viewers may be misled by overly bright areas, such as those represented in red. The concept of perceptually uniform color maps is introduced, emphasizing the need for consistent lightness distribution across the color spectrum to avoid misleading viewers and enhance the clarity of important features in the images.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So for example, what you're seeing at the top at the x-ray is that if a person is a radiologist and they want to take a look at this X-ray. They would basically take a look and say that, okay, there are bones here, they're interesting, they're interesting, but maybe they're more interested in the nuances that are happening here around where there might be some fracture in the bone or whatever. If you visualize the colors on this X-ray using jet color map, you're seeing that there is this big red area that might be distracting. So although an experience, radiologist is not going to be distracted by this kind of information. a general person might get distracted by this information. And what we're interested in is actually having more resolution for the areas that are of more interest. So the reason that you see those spikes in the red area so that it draws the attention of a viewer is because of something like this. So you would see that, OK, if I have set of intensities that I want to visualize and I visualize it using gray scale, there are some of these bright areas that are good. But if I go ahead and visualize it using jet color map, you're seeing that there are these red areas that are unnecessarily red. And then it again draws the attention of a viewer that might not be experts in evaluating what's happening in this state. The concept that we're trying to use here and figure out why that's problematic is. The concept of a color map being perceptually uniform. What that means is that if we take a look at the lightness of a color map and take a look at the range of colors that we have in that color map. And we take a look at the delta in the lightness in that color map. We also we want to make sure that the distribution is kind of uniform in a sense that, okay, if I'm going from, for example, lightness 0.1 to lightness, for example, 0.2, and I see this as specific delta. And if I go, for example, from 0.4 or 0.5 to 0.6 in this area, I should see exactly the same trend and the same like increase or decrease in the lightness. So in this case, not only that it's not increasing, but that the lightness also decreases as the color changes. So that's why you're seeing that with the jets color map, we're seeing that the lightness bounces all over the place and it's not a linear trend. But with the, with the, With our very best color map what's happening is that this loop is constant so it's a Conceptually uniform color map that we can use so not only that it's like Red green blind kind of friendly It's also going to communicate results more effectively or communicate color more effectively to the viewer So this Veritas Color Map is created by a group at Berkeley. So this is actually from Berkeley Institute of Data Science, who created this Color Map. And now the default Color Map that you're using in C-Born and Math, let lib1 from, I think, Jet Color Map to Um. We're at this column map that we have right here. So the default initially was jet column map. The other place that we can use color is when we are trying to include categories. So if I, for example, here, want to go ahead and plot different kinds of flowers based on certain attributes, I could say that go ahead and create the KDE plots for different types of colors separately and use the color channel in order to distinguish between categories. So the most effective place to use the color in the case that there is a combination of qualitative variables and quantitative variable is to visualize categories using the color in terms of visualizing qualitative variables. How can we use color more efficiently is that we can have different kinds of color schemes or color maps. So for example, in the left hand side, I'm using a color map that is sequential. So it starts from some of these high numbers from some of the high numbers to be colored as white. And then it goes all the way to this dark, this dark color that we have. And that's how we can visualize these numbers in a sequential way. There's also the type of coloring scheme that is called divergent coloring scheme. So if you want to communicate to opposite kind of views And two different specific categories that you don't want to make them sequential to incode any order, you could actually use divergent column map. So here's the column map. I think encoding different national governor suings in different states. So that's why you see some of the states be blue and some of the states be red. And it really doesn't make sense to put them on a purple sequential color scale. They should be separate colors communicating different views, specifically using the plot. Thankfully, MacPlove has a bunch of different color maps that you can directly use. You just specify the color map that you want to use. And now you know what to use at what point. So for example, here, there is the very best color map. You can take a look at the sequence. You can take a look at the range values. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

They would basically take a look and say that, okay, there are bones here, they're interesting, they're interesting, but maybe they're more interested in the nuances that are happening here around where there might be some fracture in the bone or whatever. If you visualize the colors on this X-ray using jet color map, you're seeing that there is this big red area that might be distracting. So although an experience, radiologist is not going to be distracted by this kind of information. a general person might get distracted by this information. And what we're interested in is actually having more resolution for the areas that are of more interest. So the reason that you see those spikes in the red area so that it draws the attention of a viewer is because of something like this. So you would see that, OK, if I have set of intensities that I want to visualize and I visualize it using gray scale, there are some of these bright areas that are good. But if I go ahead and visualize it using jet color map, you're seeing that there are these red areas that are unnecessarily red. And then it again draws the attention of a viewer that might not be experts in evaluating what's happening in this state. The concept that we're trying to use here and figure out why that's problematic is. The concept of a color map being perceptually uniform. What that means is that if we take a look at the lightness of a color map and take a look at the range of colors that we have in that color map. And we take a look at the delta in the lightness in that color map. We also we want to make sure that the distribution is kind of uniform in a sense that, okay, if I'm going from, for example, lightness 0.1 to lightness, for example, 0.2, and I see this as specific delta. And if I go, for example, from 0.4 or 0.5 to 0.6 in this area, I should see exactly the same trend and the same like increase or decrease in the lightness. So in this case, not only that it's not increasing, but that the lightness also decreases as the color changes. So that's why you're seeing that with the jets color map, we're seeing that the lightness bounces all over the place and it's not a linear trend. But with the, with the, With our very best color map what's happening is that this loop is constant so it's a Conceptually uniform color map that we can use so not only that it's like Red green blind kind of friendly It's also going to communicate results more effectively or communicate color more effectively to the viewer So this Veritas Color Map is created by a group at Berkeley. So this is actually from Berkeley Institute of Data Science, who created this Color Map. And now the default Color Map that you're using in C-Born and Math, let lib1 from, I think, Jet Color Map to Um. We're at this column map that we have right here. So the default initially was jet column map. The other place that we can use color is when we are trying to include categories. So if I, for example, here, want to go ahead and plot different kinds of flowers based on certain attributes, I could say that go ahead and create the KDE plots for different types of colors separately and use the color channel in order to distinguish between categories. So the most effective place to use the color in the case that there is a combination of qualitative variables and quantitative variable is to visualize categories using the color in terms of visualizing qualitative variables. How can we use color more efficiently is that we can have different kinds of color schemes or color maps. So for example, in the left hand side, I'm using a color map that is sequential. So it starts from some of these high numbers from some of the high numbers to be colored as white. And then it goes all the way to this dark, this dark color that we have. And that's how we can visualize these numbers in a sequential way. There's also the type of coloring scheme that is called divergent coloring scheme. So if you want to communicate to opposite kind of views And two different specific categories that you don't want to make them sequential to incode any order, you could actually use divergent column map. So here's the column map. I think encoding different national governor suings in different states. So that's why you see some of the states be blue and some of the states be red. And it really doesn't make sense to put them on a purple sequential color scale. They should be separate colors communicating different views, specifically using the plot. children.title: Types of Color Maps in Data Visualization
children.summary: Different types of color maps are discussed, including plasma, inferno, and magma, each with unique characteristics. Color maps can be qualitative, representing distinct categories without a specific order; sequential, which transition from light to intense colors for quantitative values; and divergent, which start and end with different colors, having white at the center to indicate neutrality. Specific examples of perceptually uniform sequential colormaps such as viridis, plasma, and inferno, as well as qualitative and diverging colormaps from matplotlib documentation, are provided.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: There's plasma, inferno, magma, and each one of them have their own kind of characteristics. And the important part is that all of them are conceptually uniform. So they don't have any issue that we had with the jet color map. We could also have color maps that are qualitative, meaning that if you have different categories that you don't want them to be sequenced in a certain way, you can pick a set of colors to represent different categories. And that's why they're called qualitative color maps. We can also have sequential color maps starting from generally in the white areas going into like intense specific colors that you can use in order to include a sequential quantitative value. We can also have divergent color maps that are starting from a certain color. They end in another color and at the center they have their all white. At the center that there's no bias into any of those divergent categories, you would get the white color when you're representing all of those categories. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

children.title: Importance of Markings in Visualization
children.summary: Marking is a crucial characteristic in visualizations, with various types such as length, slope, angle, area, color intensity, volume, and color hue. The accuracy of judgments in interpreting visual data is influenced by these markings and their specific attributes.

children.title: Importance of Markings in Visualization
children.summary: Marking is highlighted as a crucial characteristic in visualizations. Various types of markings can be used, including length, slope, angle, area, color intensity, volume, and color hue. The accuracy of our judgments in interpreting visual data is influenced by the type of marking employed.

children.title: Human Perception of Length and Area
children.summary: An exercise illustrated the differences in human perception when measuring lengths and areas. Participants estimated lengths of bars and sizes of circles, noting that one bar was seven times longer than another and one circle was seven times bigger. The findings indicated that humans find it more challenging to measure area, especially with visualizations like pie charts, emphasizing the importance of understanding these perceptual differences in data representation.

children.title: Human Perception of Length and Area
children.summary: An exercise was conducted to illustrate how human perception differs when measuring lengths and areas. Participants estimated the lengths of bars and the sizes of circles, revealing that the longer bar was seven times longer than the shorter one and the larger circle was also seven times bigger than the smaller one. The exercise demonstrated that measuring area, particularly with visualizations like pie charts, is more challenging for humans compared to measuring length, highlighting the importance of understanding these perceptual differences in data representation.

children.title: Challenges and Importance of WordCloud Visualization
children.summary: WordCloud visualizations can obscure differences between categories, complicating comparisons due to area encoding. It's essential to maintain a consistent baseline to effectively compare lengths or areas, and stacking data can further hinder clarity; thus, individual lines should be plotted separately. Accurate plotting conditions are vital for valid comparisons, such as analyzing income disparities between genders across degrees. Context is crucial in interpreting visualizations, as shown by the gender gap in education from 1970 to 2011, illustrating how time and field context affect data interpretation. Visualization aids personal understanding and reporting, exemplified by trends in women's bachelor's degree attainment.

children.title: Challenges in WordCloud Visualization and Data Comparison
children.summary: WordCloud visualizations can obscure differences between categories due to area encoding, making comparisons difficult. It's important to avoid juggling the baseline, as this hinders the ability to compare lengths or areas effectively. Stacking data complicates comparisons further; individual lines should be plotted separately for clarity. Accurate plotting conditions are crucial to ensure valid comparisons, such as analyzing income differences between men and women across various degrees. Instead of individual values, using line charts or calculated ratios can enhance visual comparisons, emphasizing the importance of feature engineering in data visualization.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Plotting is stuff using WordCloud. We're using it in the lecture. It's fun to use it. But WordCloud visualization is really hard for people to kind of see the differences between the categories because it encodes area. There are some other considerations with how to encode the channel. One important note is that we don't want you to juggle the baseline. So the juggle baseline means that for all of these bars, the blue region is kind of of the similar lengths or the similar area also. But we are not able to compare them because we're juggling the baseline using all of the additional kind of categories that we're plotting. the same thing with the X dimension, it doesn't matter if you juggle the baseline, it's really hard to to compare categories with each other. We also don't like to stack this stuff on top of each other. So here it's really hard to compare the lengths when there is stack to each other as opposed to just looking at each one of those individual lines plotted separately. So this is also another thing to consider. With the conditioning a lot of time we want to make sure that the conditions that we want to plot are plotted accurately and we're comparing apples to apples. So this is an example that if I want to take a look at the differences in incomes for men and women for different degrees that they have, this is not a good way of comparing that condition because you're not going to be able to compare this red bar with this red bar more efficiently. What you can do instead is to have a line chart for each one of them so that you can compare which one of them is bigger than the other one kind of more visually. We could also get more fancier and calculate the ratio. This is why feature engineering is really important that instead of plotting individual values, plot the variation of it or the engineered version of those values that we have. We could also include condition using juxtaposition as the post to super position super position is that you plot multiple things on the same plot. We could also put different conditions side by side is called a small multiples that we're plotting so that for different conditions you're plotting different things side by side so that you can compare them more accurately. leaf_chunk.audio: Plotting is stuff using WordCloud. We're using it in the lecture. It's fun to use it. But WordCloud visualization is really hard for people to kind of see the differences between the categories because it encodes area. There are some other considerations with how to encode the channel. One important note is that we don't want you to juggle the baseline. So the juggle baseline means that for all of these bars, the blue region is kind of of the similar lengths or the similar area also. But we are not able to compare them because we're juggling the baseline using all of the additional kind of categories that we're plotting. the same thing with the X dimension, it doesn't matter if you juggle the baseline, it's really hard to to compare categories with each other. We also don't like to stack this stuff on top of each other. So here it's really hard to compare the lengths when there is stack to each other as opposed to just looking at each one of those individual lines plotted separately. So this is also another thing to consider. With the conditioning a lot of time we want to make sure that the conditions that we want to plot are plotted accurately and we're comparing apples to apples. So this is an example that if I want to take a look at the differences in incomes for men and women for different degrees that they have, this is not a good way of comparing that condition because you're not going to be able to compare this red bar with this red bar more efficiently. What you can do instead is to have a line chart for each one of them so that you can compare which one of them is bigger than the other one kind of more visually. We could also get more fancier and calculate the ratio. This is why feature engineering is really important that instead of plotting individual values, plot the variation of it or the engineered version of those values that we have. We could also include condition using juxtaposition as the post to super position super position is that you plot multiple things on the same plot. We could also put different conditions side by side is called a small multiples that we're plotting so that for different conditions you're plotting different things side by side so that you can compare them more accurately.

children.title: Importance of Context in Data Visualization
children.summary: Context is crucial when interpreting data visualizations, particularly in understanding trends and relationships. The lecture highlights the example of the gender gap in education, showcasing the percentage of bachelor's degrees conferred to women in various fields from 1970 to 2011. It emphasizes that the gender gap is transitory, even in extreme cases where women's representation was below 20% in 1970, illustrating how the context of time and field influences the interpretation of data.

children.title: Reasons for Visualization in Data Analysis
children.summary: Visualization serves two main purposes: it aids personal understanding of data, referred to as ETA (Estimated Time of Arrival), and it facilitates reporting. The discussion includes a specific example of the gender gap in education, highlighting trends in the percentage of bachelor's degrees conferred to women across various fields from 1970 to 2011, using a graph to illustrate changes over time.

children.title: Creating Publication-Ready Plots Essentials
children.summary: Effective publication-ready plots should utilize all visual channels efficiently, featuring clear X and Y labels, informative titles that convey main takeaways, and legends when necessary. Essential components include reference lines, markers, and labels for significant values. For example, a title like 'Older passengers spend more on plane tickets' is preferred over a mere description. All plots in the course must include these elements, along with captions that describe the data.

children.title: Creating Publication-Ready Plots
children.summary: To create effective plots for reporting, it's important to use all visual channels efficiently. Each plot should include clear X and Y labels, a descriptive title that conveys the main takeaway, and a legend if necessary. Additionally, plots should incorporate reference lines, markers, and labels for significant values. In this course, all plots must have informative titles and axis labels, along with captions that describe the data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: If you do it for yourself, it can be lightweight. It can be just a simple, not so nice map-lovely plot that you're creating. If you're trying to plot something for reporting, then you need to consider using all the channels more efficiently, have every plot that you have X label, Y label, title, nice, also legend on all of the plots and so on and so forth. children.title: Creating Publication-Ready Plots
children.summary: Key elements for creating publication-ready plots include having an informative title that conveys a takeaway message, such as 'Older passengers spend more on plane tickets,' rather than a descriptive title. Essential components also include axis labels, reference lines and markers for important values, appropriate legends, and captions that describe the data. All plots created in the course must include titles and axis labels.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Kind of as a takeaway point is that now you know how to create different plots, how to efficiently plot different variables, how to plot association between different variables, and then how to use all the information channels that you have available on the canvas of the plot more efficiently and consider color is scaling someone and so forth.
